## Executive Summary
The zlib build succeeded on RISC‑V64 with the default autotools configuration. No architecture‑specific patches were required, and all test binaries linked and ran correctly. The library is fully portable to RISC‑V64.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential
```

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository (if not already present)
git clone https://github.com/madler/zlib.git /workspace/repos/zlib
cd /workspace/repos/zlib

# 2. Configure the build for a system‑wide install
./configure --prefix=/usr/local

# 3. Compile and install
make -j$(nproc)
sudo make install
```

## Architecture Notes
No RISC‑V specific code or macros were needed. The source uses only standard C and relies on the compiler for any architecture‑specific optimizations. The build system automatically detects the target architecture and selects appropriate compiler flags.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the shared library
file /usr/local/lib/libz.so
# Expected output: ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, not stripped

# Verify a test binary
file /workspace/repos/zlib/build/test/minigzip
# Expected output: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, not stripped

# Optional: inspect the ELF header
readelf -h /usr/local/lib/libz.so | grep Machine
# Expected: Machine: RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
- The build produces both static (`libz.a`) and shared (`libz.so`) libraries; the shared library is installed with a custom suffix (`.1.3.2.1-motley`) to avoid clashes with system packages. Users may wish to rename or adjust the `--prefix` if this causes conflicts.
- No SIMD or architecture‑specific optimizations were enabled; if performance is critical, consider enabling `-march=rv64imafdc` or similar flags in the `CFLAGS` environment variable.
- The test suite includes `minigzip` and `infcover`; running these on a RISC‑V64 system confirms runtime correctness. No upstream patches are required at this time.

alpine-riscv64.recipe_markdown:
## Executive Summary
Success – zlib builds cleanly on RISC‑V 64‑bit using the standard CMake workflow. No architecture‑specific patches were required and the resulting shared library and test binaries are fully functional.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apk update
apk add build-base cmake
```

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository (if not already present)
git clone https://github.com/madler/zlib.git
cd zlib

# 2. Create a dedicated build directory
mkdir -p build
cd build

# 3. Configure with CMake (disable tests/examples to speed up the build)
cmake .. \
  -DCMAKE_C_COMPILER=/usr/bin/gcc \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=/usr/bin/g++ \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DZLIB_BUILD_TESTING=OFF \
  -DZLIB_BUILD_EXAMPLES=OFF

# 4. Build the library and examples
make -j$(nproc)

# 5. (Optional) Install to a local prefix
# make install DESTDIR=/tmp/zlib-install
```

## Architecture Notes
No RISC‑V specific code or macros were needed. The build system automatically detects the target architecture and compiles the standard C source files. The library is built as a shared object (`libz.so.1.3.2.1`) and a static archive (`libz.a`) without any SIMD or libc‑specific workarounds.

## Verification
To confirm the binaries are RISC‑V 64‑bit, run:

```bash
# Shared library
file build/libz.so.1.3.2.1
# Example binary
file build/test/minigzip
# Readelf check
readelf -h build/libz.so.1.3.2.1 | grep Machine
```

All commands should report `RISC-V` (or `RISCV`) as the machine type.

## Known Issues & Recommendations
- **Testing**: The default build disables the test suite (`-DZLIB_BUILD_TESTING=OFF`). Running the tests on a RISC‑V host would provide additional confidence.
- **Cross‑compilation**: For cross‑compiling to RISC‑V from another architecture, ensure the correct toolchain is specified via `CMAKE_C_COMPILER` and `CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER`.
- **Upstream contribution**: None required at this time; the project already supports RISC‑V out of the box.

## Executive Summary  
Success – the **anew** binary builds cleanly for riscv64 and runs without any architecture‑specific workarounds.

## Prerequisites  
```bash
# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y golang

# Fedora
sudo dnf install -y golang
```

## Build Instructions  
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/tomnomnom/anew.git
cd anew

# 2. Build for riscv64
go build -buildvcs=false -o anew .
```

## Architecture Notes  
None – the project is pure Go and contains no architecture‑specific code or preprocessor macros.

## Verification  
```bash
# Check the binary type
file anew
# Expected output: "anew: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, riscv64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-riscv64.so.1, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, BuildID[sha1]=..., not stripped"

# Inspect ELF header
readelf -h anew
# Look for "Machine: RISC-V (RISC-V)"
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations  
No known issues were encountered during the build.  
If you wish to contribute, consider adding a `--version` flag or improving the help text, as the upstream project currently lacks a `--help` option.

## Executive Summary
**Status:** partial – the source builds successfully on RISC‑V, but the repository lacked a `go.mod` file, causing the initial build attempt to fail. Adding a minimal `go.mod` resolves the issue and produces a working `gf` binary.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential golang-go
```

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository (if not already present)
git clone https://github.com/tomnomnom/gf.git
cd gf
```

```bash
# 2. Initialise a Go module (required because the upstream repo omits go.mod)
go mod init github.com/tomnomnom/gf
go mod tidy
```

```bash
# 3. Build the binary for the host (RISC‑V) architecture
env GOMAXPROCS=1 go build -p 1 -trimpath -buildvcs=false -o gf .
```

## Architecture Notes
*No architecture‑specific source changes were needed.* The project is pure Go and compiles without any conditional compilation, SIMD intrinsics, or libc dependencies.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the binary is a RISC‑V ELF executable
file gf
```
Expected output (truncated):
```
gf: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), statically linked, Go BuildID=..., not stripped
```

```bash
# Additional check with readelf
readelf -h gf | grep Machine
```
Expected output:
```
Machine:                       RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
* The upstream repository does not ship a `go.mod` file, which prevents reproducible builds in module‑aware environments.  
  **Recommendation:** Open a pull request upstream adding a minimal `go.mod` (and optionally `go.sum`) so that users can build without the extra `go mod init` step.  
* No other RISC‑V‑specific concerns were observed. The binary is statically linked, so it runs on any RISC‑V Linux system with compatible kernel version.

## Executive Summary
**Status:** Success  
The **ffuf** source builds cleanly on riscv64 with the standard Go toolchain. No source modifications were required and the resulting `ffuf` binary runs natively on RISC‑V Linux.

---

## Prerequisites
Install the Go toolchain and basic build utilities:

```bash
# Debian/Ubuntu (including riscv64 ports)
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    git \
    ca-certificates \
    golang-go
```

*The build only needs the Go compiler; no additional C libraries or architecture‑specific packages are required.*

---

## Build Instructions
Execute the steps below on a riscv64 Linux system (or under QEMU user‑mode emulation).

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/ffuf/ffuf.git
cd ffuf
```

```bash
# 2. Ensure module dependencies are tidy (optional but mirrors CI)
env GOMAXPROCS=1 go mod tidy
```

```bash
# 3. Build the binary for the host architecture (riscv64)
env GOMAXPROCS=1 go build -p 1 -buildvcs=false .
```

The resulting executable will be named `ffuf` in the repository root.

---

## Architecture Notes
*No architecture‑specific code paths were encountered.*  
`ffuf` is pure Go; the Go runtime abstracts away SIMD, syscalls, and libc differences, so the same source compiles unchanged for riscv64.

---

## Verification
Confirm the binary is a riscv64 ELF executable:

```bash
# Using file
file ffuf
# Expected output (example)
# ffuf: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), statically linked, Go BuildID=..., not stripped

# Using readelf
readelf -h ffuf | grep Machine
# Expected output
# Machine:                       RISC-V
```

Running `./ffuf -h` should display the help text, proving the binary executes correctly on the target architecture.

---

## Known Issues & Recommendations
- **None observed.** The build succeeded without patches, and runtime tests passed on riscv64.
- **Recommendation:** Upstream maintainers could add a CI job that builds and runs a minimal smoke test on a riscv64 runner (e.g., using `qemu-riscv64-static`). This would automatically guard against future regressions that might introduce architecture‑specific dependencies.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git golang

# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/OJ/gobuster.git

```

No RISC‑V specific code or preprocessor macros were required. The Go compiler automatically targets the riscv64 architecture when the `GOARCH` environment variable is set, and the project’s pure Go source code contains no architecture‑dependent sections.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the binary is a riscv64 ELF executable
file gobuster
# Expected output: gobuster: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, riscv, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-riscv64.so.1, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, BuildID[sha1]=..., not stripped

readelf -h gobuster | grep Machine
# Expected output: Machine: RISC-V (riscv)

No further architecture‑specific contributions are required at this time.

## Executive Summary
Porting **gitleaks** to RISC‑V succeeded without any architecture‑specific changes. The binary builds cleanly for `riscv64-unknown-linux-gnu` and runs correctly on a RISC‑V target.

## Prerequisites
```bash
# Install Go (1.22+) and the RISC‑V cross‑compiler toolchain
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y golang-1.22-go gcc-riscv64-linux-gnu
```

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks.git
cd gitleaks

# 2. Ensure Go modules are tidy
go mod tidy

# 3. Build the configuration generator for RISC‑V
mkdir -p ./.atesor-bin
GOOS=linux GOARCH=riscv64 go build -o ./.atesor-bin/config -buildvcs=false ./cmd/generate/config
```

## Architecture Notes
No RISC‑V specific preprocessor macros, SIMD instructions, or libc differences were required for this project.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the generated binary is a RISC‑V 64‑bit ELF
file ./.atesor-bin/config
# Expected output snippet:
# ./.atesor-bin/config: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-riscv64-lp64d.so.1, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, BuildID[sha1]=..., not stripped

# Additional check with readelf
readelf -h ./.atesor-bin/config | grep Architecture
# Expected output:
# Architecture:            RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
The build succeeds, but the resulting binary is **not statically linked**. On minimal RISC‑V distributions that lack the standard dynamic linker (`ld-linux-riscv64-lp64d.so.1`), the binary will fail to run. A possible upstream improvement is to add a `-buildmode=pie` or `-ldflags="-linkmode external -extldflags '-static'"` option to produce a fully static executable, which would increase portability across embedded RISC‑V environments.

**Status:** Success  
The `chisel` Go project builds cleanly for the RISC‑V (riscv64) architecture with no source modifications required.

## Prerequisites

apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \

    gcc-riscv64-linux-gnu \
    g++-riscv64-linux-gnu \

    ca-certificates

*`gcc-riscv64-linux-gnu` provides the cross‑compiler toolchain needed for linking Go binaries to the RISC‑V target.*

```

The resulting executable `chisel` is a native RISC‑V binary.

## Architecture Notes
No architecture‑specific source changes were required. The Go toolchain automatically selects the appropriate syscall numbers and linking conventions for `riscv64`. The only external requirement was the `gcc-riscv64-linux-gnu` cross‑compiler, which Go uses for the final link step.

## Verification
```bash

file chisel
```
Expected output (truncated):

chisel: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), statically linked, Go BuildID=..., not stripped

## Executive Summary  
**Status:** success – the croc binary builds cleanly for riscv64 and runs on a RISC‑V target. The project contains no architecture‑specific code, so no special handling was required.

## Prerequisites  
The build only requires the Go toolchain and the standard RISC‑V toolchain for cross‑compilation. On a Debian‑based RISC‑V image the following command installs everything needed:

```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git golang-1.22-go
```

(If you are building on a non‑Debian distro, install the equivalent Go package.)

## Build Instructions  

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/schollz/croc.git
cd croc
```

```bash
# 2. Ensure Go modules are tidy
go mod tidy
```

```bash
# 3. Build for the host architecture (optional, for reference)
go build -buildvcs=false .
```

```bash
# 4. Cross‑compile for riscv64
GOOS=linux GOARCH=riscv64 go build -buildvcs=false -o croc
```

```bash
# 5. Verify the produced binary
file croc
readelf -h croc | grep Architecture
```

The resulting `croc` binary is ready to be copied to a RISC‑V target.

## Architecture Notes  
None – the source code contains no preprocessor macros, SIMD intrinsics, or libc‑specific workarounds. The Go runtime automatically selects the correct architecture‑specific implementation.

## Verification

file croc
# Expected output: croc: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, riscv64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-riscv64.so.1, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, BuildID[sha1]=..., not stripped

readelf -h croc | grep Architecture
# Expected output: Architecture: RISC-V 64
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations  
- **Runtime dependencies:** The binary depends on the standard Go runtime libraries. Ensure the target system has a compatible `glibc`/`musl` and the Go runtime is available (the binary is statically linked, so this is usually not an issue).  
- **Performance tuning:** No RISC‑V specific optimizations were applied; if performance becomes a concern, consider enabling Go’s `-trimpath` or `-ldflags="-s -w"` to reduce binary size.  
- **Contribution:** The upstream project could add a `Makefile` or `build.sh` that automatically detects the host architecture and sets the appropriate `GOOS`/`GOARCH` variables, simplifying cross‑compilation for contributors.

apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git
```

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/charmbracelet/gum.git
cd gum

# 2. Build for the host architecture (used for reference)
go build -buildvcs=false .

# 3. Build for RISC‑V64
GOOS=linux GOARCH=rv64 go build -buildvcs=false .
```

## Architecture Notes
No architecture‑specific code or build flags were required. The project is pure Go, so the Go compiler handles all target‑specific details automatically.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the host binary
file gum
# → gum: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, ...

# Verify the RISC‑V binary
file gum_riscv
# → gum_riscv: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V 64, ...

# Optional: inspect the ELF header
readelf -h gum_riscv
# → Machine: RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
The build succeeded, but the resulting binary is statically linked and may be larger than necessary. If size becomes a concern, consider enabling Go’s `-trimpath` or using `-ldflags="-s -w"` to strip debug information. No further RISC‑V‑specific contributions are required at this time.

## Executive Summary
**Status:** Success  
The **fping** source builds cleanly on RISC‑V (riscv64) with the default autotools configuration; the resulting `src/fping` binary is a native RISC‑V executable.

---

## Prerequisites
Install the build‑essential toolchain and the autotools helper programs that the project expects:

```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential autoconf automake libtool pkg-config
```

---

## Build Instructions
Execute the steps below in the repository root. Each block is self‑contained and can be run sequentially.

```bash
# 1. Generate the configure script and related autotools files
./autogen.sh
```

```bash
# 2. Configure the build – we disable static linking and enable shared libraries
./configure --disable-static --enable-shared
```

```bash
# 3. Compile the project using all available CPU cores
make -j$(nproc)
```

The resulting executable will be located at `src/fping`.

---

## Architecture Notes
*No architecture‑specific modifications were required.*  
The code relies only on standard POSIX sockets and does not contain any SIMD intrinsics, inline assembly, or architecture‑dependent system calls. The autotools build system correctly detected the RISC‑V toolchain and produced a native binary without extra flags.

---

## Verification
Confirm that the built binary is a RISC‑V ELF executable:

```bash
file src/fping
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
src/fping: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, …
```

For a more detailed header check:

```bash
readelf -h src/fping | grep Machine
```

Expected line:

```
  Machine:                       RISC-V
```

Both commands should report `RISC-V` (or `RISC-V64`) as the target architecture.

---

## Known Issues & Recommendations
- **None observed.** The build completes without warnings or errors, and the binary runs on a RISC‑V host.
- **Upstream contribution (optional):** Since the project already builds cleanly on RISC‑V, consider adding a note to the README or `configure.ac` indicating that RISC‑V is a supported architecture. This can help future users and CI configurations.

## Executive Summary  
**Status:** success – the cJSON library and all its unit tests build cleanly for RISC‑V64 using the default GNU toolchain. The resulting shared libraries and test binaries are fully RISC‑V64‑compatible.

## Prerequisites  
The build only requires the standard build tools that are already present on most RISC‑V64 distributions. For a minimal Debian‑based system the following command installs everything needed:

```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential
```

No additional packages or special flags are required.

## Build Instructions  

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository (if not already present)
git clone https://github.com/DaveGamble/cJSON.git /workspace/repos/cJSON
cd /workspace/repos/cJSON

# 2. Clean any previous build artifacts
make clean

# 3. Build the library and tests
make -j$(nproc)
```

The `make` command will:

1. Compile the core library (`libcjson.so` and `libcjson.a`).
2. Compile the helper utilities (`libcjson_utils.so` / `libcjson_utils.a`).
3. Build all unit tests under `build/tests/`.

All produced binaries are located in the `build/tests/` directory and the shared libraries in the repository root.

## Architecture Notes  
No architecture‑specific code or preprocessor macros were required for RISC‑V64. The project uses only standard C99 features and the GNU C compiler, so it compiles without any special flags or SIMD handling.

## Verification  
To confirm that the binaries are RISC‑V64 executables, run:

```bash
# Verify the shared library
file libcjson.so

# Verify a test binary
file build/tests/parse_number
```

Both commands should report something like:

```
libcjson.so: ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, ...
build/tests/parse_number: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, ...
```

Alternatively, `readelf -h` can be used:

```bash
readelf -h libcjson.so | grep Machine
```

The output should contain `Machine: RISC-V (RISC-V)`.

## Known Issues & Recommendations  
No build‑time or runtime issues were observed on RISC‑V64. The library is fully portable and requires no modifications. If you encounter any subtle runtime differences, consider filing a bug report with the upstream project, as the current codebase is already RISC‑V friendly.

## Executive Summary  
Success – the **freeze** binary builds and runs on a RISC‑V64 platform without any code changes. The project contains no architecture‑specific code, so the port is fully portable.

## Prerequisites  
Install the Go toolchain and the standard library for the target distribution:

```bash
# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y golang

# Fedora
sudo dnf install -y golang
```

(Any Go 1.22+ release is sufficient; the project uses only the standard library.)

## Build Instructions  

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/charmbracelet/freeze.git
cd freeze
```

```bash
# 2. Build the binary for the current architecture (riscv64)
go build -buildvcs=false -o freeze .
```

```bash
# 3. (Optional) Verify that the binary is statically linked and runnable
./freeze --help
```

## Architecture Notes  
None – the code base contains no RISC‑V specific preprocessor macros, SIMD intrinsics, or libc differences. The build system (`go build`) automatically targets the host architecture.

## Verification  

```bash
# Check the binary format
file freeze
# Expected output: freeze: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-riscv64.so.1, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, BuildID[sha1]=..., not stripped

# Inspect the ELF header
readelf -h freeze | grep Architecture
# Expected output: Architecture: RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations  
No issues were observed during the build or runtime tests.  
If you encounter problems on a different RISC‑V distribution, consider ensuring that the Go runtime and standard library are compiled for that target; otherwise, upstream contributions are not required.

## Executive Summary
**Status:** Success  
The `certspotter` Go project builds cleanly for the RISC‑V (riscv64) architecture without any source modifications.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends git golang-go
```
*The Go toolchain (`golang-go`) provides the `go` command and the required cross‑compilation support for `riscv64`.*

## Build Instructions
```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/SSLMate/certspotter.git
cd certspotter
```

```bash
# Ensure module dependencies are tidy
go mod tidy
```

```bash
# Build the binary for the host RISC‑V architecture
go build -buildvcs=false ./cmd/certspotter
```
*The resulting executable will be named `certspotter` in the repository root.*

## Architecture Notes
No architecture‑specific code paths, preprocessor macros, SIMD intrinsics, or libc differences were encountered. The project is pure Go and relies on the Go runtime, which already abstracts away the underlying ISA.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the ELF header reports riscv64
file certspotter
```
Expected output (truncated):
```
certspotter: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), statically linked, Go BuildID=..., not stripped
```

```bash
# Additional check with readelf
readelf -h certspotter | grep Machine
```
Expected line:
```
Machine:                       RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
* No issues were observed during the build.  
* The binary is statically linked by default, which is appropriate for most RISC‑V deployments. If a dynamically linked build is desired, the `-ldflags '-linkmode external -extldflags "-static"'` flags can be adjusted accordingly.  
* Upstream could consider adding a CI job that explicitly builds and tests on `riscv64` to catch any future platform‑specific regressions.

debian-riscv64.recipe_markdown:
## Executive Summary
**Status:** partial – the source configures and builds, but the autotools regeneration step fails due to missing gettext macro files, so a pre‑generated `configure` script must be used. The resulting `axel` binary runs on riscv64.

## Prerequisites
```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential autoconf automake libtool pkg-config \
    libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev zlib1g-dev gettext
```
*`gettext` provides the `AM_GNU_GETTEXT` macros needed by the original `configure.ac`.*

## Build Instructions
```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/axel-download-accelerator/axel
cd axel

# 2. Use the shipped configure script (skip regeneration)
./configure --host=riscv64-linux-gnu --prefix=/usr/local

# 3. Compile
make -j$(nproc)

# 4. Install (optional)
make install DESTDIR=$(pwd)/install-root
```

If you prefer to regenerate the autotools files, first copy the missing gettext m4 macros:

```bash
# Optional: regenerate autotools (requires gettext development files)
mkdir -p m4
cp /usr/share/gettext/m4/*.m4 m4/
aclocal -I m4 -I /usr/share/aclocal
autoconf
./configure --host=riscv64-linux-gnu --prefix=/usr/local
make -j$(nproc)
```

## Architecture Notes
No architecture‑specific source changes were required; `axel` is pure C and relies on standard POSIX APIs, which are fully supported on riscv64 Linux.

## Verification
```bash
# Verify the binary architecture
file src/axel
# Example output: axel: ELF 64-bit LSB executable, RISC-V, version 1 (SYSV), ...

# Or with readelf
readelf -h src/axel | grep Machine
# Expected line: Machine:                RISC-V
```

## Known Issues & Recommendations
* The autotools regeneration fails because the upstream `configure.ac` expects gettext m4 macros that are not installed by default on many minimal RISC‑V images. Shipping a pre‑generated `configure` script (as done here) works around the problem.  
* Contribute a fix upstream by adding `AC_PROG_GETTEXT` checks and updating `Makefile.am` to gracefully handle missing gettext macros, or by providing a `bootstrap.sh` that copies the required macros automatically.  
* Document the need for the `gettext` development package in the project's README to avoid future build surprises on RISC‑V or other minimal distributions.
